speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor's name is George Gottlieb. The date is September 24, 1996, tape three. Mr. Gottlieb, we were talking about being on a cattle train, leaving your ghetto. Let me ask you, what were you told before getting on a cattle train? What explanations were given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No explanation, with the exception of, once again, coming back to the famous world of rumor. It's strictly rumors. It's say so. There was really no explanation. There was no such a thing as an official coming over, Hungarian or German military officer would say that you are going x place or doing x, or nothing. I mean, just like cattle, like sheep, we're-- we're pushed into that car on the guards, of SS guards with machine guns. And there was no such a thing as you disobey or you do anything. Because they had ruthlessly, right in front of us, anybody who tried to disobey, they were just killed. Gunned down.

speaker: interviewer
text: So basically, the only knowledge, the only rumor what we had, once again, we are going to go work somehow, somewhere, for the army. Some kind of labor camp, somewhere where they are going to use us as-- as the slave labor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as soon as the door closed, and as soon as the-- the train started to move, just by knowing the country's geography, we knew that we are heading towards the Northeast. We're heading north and mostly east. And it became quite obvious that we are going towards the Russian front. We are going towards the Russian-- where basically, the only place for the Hungarian army was there. And-- and-- and it was, to a certain extent, like a-- not a relief. But at-- but-- but at least we know where, what we are doing. That was the rumor. Then suddenly, we saw that the train is out of Hungary. Now we are in Poland. And the train in between never stopped for any other reason but for a day-- or a-- for a-- for a 1/2 an hour or 3/4 of an hour, somewhere in an unknown-- or not even on a station, just on the tracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had no-- second day went by. And then finally, on the third day, we were deep in Poland. We knew we are in Poland. And during all this time, we had no such a thing as nobody cared, if we have water, food, anything. But naturally, I mean, I don't have to tell you that. Because it's-- it's-- it's still coming from the-- still coming from a-- from the ghetto that every single family had what to eat, something to eat, or something to-- to munch on, or something to-- to keep you alive. And finally, the third day-- memory wise, the third day in the morning-- 'cause it's either the morning, noon time-- the train came in a screeching halt. And now something, what I would like to bring to-- I don't know if the proper time or place to tell you, that it was-- on the train there was a conversation between dad and another gentleman. And his closest friend was a-- was our family doctor and his closest friend. And that is unbelievable, that during that-- that time when it was quite obvious that every single effort required and needed for the war effort, that so many SS guard were able to guard us. And so many train, a huge, big train was ever able to carry us instead of carrying some kind of material to the front. It was just-- it sticks in my mind, even today, that that conversation, that this is something which the expression where they can say it's not kosher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why does it so much effort, and so much SS guard, and so much military guard? And basically had that whole big trainload. And that, later on crystallizing, when finally the train stopped and the doors swung open.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first thing, what still sticks in my mind, is the tremendous stench. The smell was just horrendous. We had no idea where we are. We had no idea where we are coming to.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to back up for a moment. You were discussing the conversation in the cattle car. Could you clarify that conversation, and-- and tell me, again, what was going on? What was going on, the-- the-- the back and forth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the basic conversation was that really, I mean, if they are taking us to so-called labor camp, why are the older people, why are the woman there? Why are babies there?

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, where are we going? Or what is their aim? What is their aim? To re-transplant everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean-- I mean, we never heard about what later on I-- we found out the Final Solution was, and et cetera. That word was unknown. It was totally unknown to us, why is this tremendous effort to displace so many Jews from their home, from their-- from their area, from their-- even productive life. And this was basically the discussions between my father and three-- three or four people who were close by in a corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about fear? What about fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had very, very horrifying-- even today to think about it. Everybody had a basic fate or-- or no fear, but basic fate in the Hungarian government that they're not going to allow to do anything to Hungarian citizens. Once again, coming back, we were Hungarians, but Jewish religion. And somehow, somewhere, the faith was there that the government will never allow that anything dramatic should happen to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we're back. The train stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: The train stopped. And as I've said, the first, first thing is the stench. As the doors open, we were quite happy. First time we thought that-- not only thought. We actually would be able to stretch our legs out and walk. Because in that-- in that car we were like sardines, compacted. Very difficult to walk. Very difficult to sit. Or very difficult to do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: About how many were in a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 70 to 100. Huge. I mean, packed like sardines, really, one on top of another. I mean, there was no such thing as lying down or no such a thing as-- you sat on your-- on your-- on your suitcase. As many suitcases they were able to packed in, as many people coming. And a little room for your leg. But that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any sickness on the train, of the people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing that I remember major. I mean, no major sickness. It was the older people, especially older ladies, who were crying and you know, the oy vey and et cetera, which is expected.

speaker: interviewer
text: The stench that you smelled was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the only thing is what I have to say, that I think that both my brother and I, we were already hardened. And were already hardened, what happened to us for the previous 2 and 1/2 months. And we were basically, I think, some of the-- the best soldiers on that train. But the stench was tremendous when the door opened. But we jumped off. And there were people, SS guards, a tremendous amount of German shepherds. And for the first time, what we saw, that people in a striped uniform. The striped uniform was a blue-and-gray stripe, where prisoners, it was visualized that they are some kind of a prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they look like, other than the stripe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they had the striped jacket, the striped pants, the striped beret and striped shoe. They-- they looked like normal human beings. And they're in all kinds of languages, including Hungarian. We received the instructions that men and women separately, form a line of five, and march forwards.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this was, basically, the first time that dad was holding onto grandpa. And then my brother. Then I think so I was in the middle. And then my brother on the outside, that we were separated from mom.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was yelling and it's crying and screaming. And mom was holding on to one of her sister-in-laws, and one of her-- two of her sisters. So they're-- at least the four of them were together. And man and woman went separately, and march forwards in a line of five. And that famous line of five was with us for the rest of my-- after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean by line of five? They-- they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: You had to be line of five. Five people in a line, and march forwards. You-- you couldn't be six. You couldn't be four. It's five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The [INAUDIBLE] basically, leave all your belonging in the train. We are going to take it off. You go forwards and march forwards.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, basically where what belonging? I mean, whatever belongings, couldn't care less. But basically, you follow the instructions. We march forwards and-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --it came to the head of the line.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the head of the line, there was three German officers. The officer in the middle-- and I will describe to you later how I know everything so intimately-- was Dr. Mengele. M-E-N-G-E-L-E, Dr. Mengele was the famous doctor who, in the name of medicine and in the name of Final Solution and-- and et cetera, committed probably the biggest crime.

speaker: interviewer
text: I don't know if his crime wasn't bigger than-- than anybody else in-- in-- in-- in Nazi history. Dr. Mengele had only one question, [GERMAN], are you a twin? And anybody who was any type of multiple birth immediately went separate, with separate SS guards, the so-called [GERMAN], which is the twins' camp, where experiments were done to male and females, without any kind of anesthetic or anything. And where they removed the republic-- the-- the reproductive organs, without anything, regardless if the person will survive or die, to try to find out how can they multiply the perfect German population? How can they obtain multiple births? Now, if this is medicine or this is humanity, then he was way below that. Were you aware of what his purpose was when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: None. At that time, only we know one thing. That Dr. Mengele, the same time with his thumb, decided life and death. If he-- his thumb went to his right, that means to your left, you automatically went to the crematoriums, the gas chambers, what we had no idea they exist. Till then, we had no idea where we are. If you went to the left, you went on the living side. Immediately right there, dad and grandpa was separated from us. They went to one side. My brother and I went to the other side. I assume I went with my brother only because he was bigger, taller, stronger. And also, he held extremely tight onto me. So I was pushed that way. Grandpa and dad went on the other way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: No idea. That was woman. Woman went on one row. We went on other row. We had no idea. We'd lost complete sight of her, complete even ear sight of where-- what happened to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Mengele speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a-- the only question, what the SS officers ask, are [GERMAN]? Are you a-- a twin? They ask in Hungarian too. One guy ask in Hungarian. Are you a twin? No other word, absolutely none. It's strictly the finger, the thumb.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any demeanor of Mengele? Any-- any revealing demeanor that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was just a tall, very good-looking very, very good-looking, tall German officer, who-- we knew it from the uniform-- that he was-- he was a-- a medic. He was in the medical profession.

speaker: interviewee
text: But just that-- how do you call it? A stone-faced nothing. And from there, we were marched into-- my brother and I are holding onto each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought, at that time, that here is the famous separation where dad and grandpa are not going to be able to work as hard as him and me. In our mind, that's what's happening right now. That's the reason why we were separated. That the older people, all the middle-aged and older people went one way. We went the other way, to the harder labor camp, or whatever they were able to do. We were ushered into a-- little barracks, little rooms. There were not bigger than, I don't know, 30 by 30 feet, 30 feet by 30 feet. So let's say 1,000 square feet. I have no idea. Well, the instruction was get undressed. Because you are going to be disinfected, cleaned and disinfected. Keep your clothes in a nice pile, and onto the next room.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get undressed, onto the next room. Well, the next room, all human hair was removed. And I don't have to-- you understand, that anywhere where human hair grows, with a shear it was removed.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hair from underarm, hair over the head, and like I said, wherever hair grows. And then onto the next room. And the next room, they asked the question that-- which I found that-- I mean, I was, once again, just too young to understand what's happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: All personal belongings there, they ask for. So I looked at my brother. And he looked at me, both of us, with a smile on our face. You know? Like how do we look, bald and-- and shaven all over? And what personal belonging?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we realized that I had a watch. He had a watch. We had no rings, none of us. None of us wore glasses. But they took away the watches. They took away the-- they took away the rings, if somebody had. And somebody had glasses, they took away the glasses. And the most brutal part was the next step. That before you went to the next room, you had to open your mouth. And everybody who had a gold teeth or a gold filling, with a pair of pliers it was removed. And onto the next room. At the next room you received your gorgeous uniform which consisted of a striped jacket, pants, a shirt, a beret, no underwear, and a shoe which was the same canvas, the same striped canvas with a wooden sole. And then towards the door of going out with the unbelievable precision of the Nazis, you had to go in front of a table where you had to give your name, your date and place of birth, naturally, your nationality. And then on that uniform there was a number. Right next to the number there was a yellow star. The yellow star meant that you were a Jew. The number-- and they told you right there at that table that from now on you live by that number. You have no more name.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: 6-7-1-5-9. With a B at the beginning, B for-- at that time, we found out the B stands for Birkenau. So we arrived to Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the yellow star? Did it have any [INAUDIBLE] on it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But you have to understand that the yellow star was tiny. No more than probably an inch tall, an inch wide. We learned after, in camp, that there are different markings. Beside of my number on that little-- on that little triangle, the worst was a yellow and the red triangle. The yellow triangle make you a Jew. The red triangle meant that you are a political prisoner. A black triangle meant that you are a gypsy. A pink triangle meant that you are homosexual. I mean, on the men's side. A green triangle meant that you are a Jehovah Witness. Everybody had his own ID. So that when the SS looked at you, they knew exactly not only that what's your number, what's the reason why you are there in Auschwitz-Birkenau. Auschwitz-Birkenau had a large population of prisoners, not only Jews. But it was mostly Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Question that I wanted to ask you, the year? Will-- would you please give me the year that you were there? The-- the month and the year that you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in end of May, 1944, to Auschwitz-Birkenau. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what we found out, only is Birkenau. And at that time, once we get ready for your-- this number which was given to you, that was the number what most of the people received as a tattoo in their arm, the left arm, inside or outside. The number on your uniform for A or B. A if you went to Auschwitz camp, B if you went to the Birkenau camp. For a long, long time I had no idea why I haven't got a tattoo. None. My-- even my children, they ask me. And I use the-- the-- the joke that probably the Germans ran out of ink. I really had no idea why. I knew only one thing. That the time when they-- when I gave my name, address, and the main place of birth, et cetera, suddenly my brother gave it too and he gave me a shove, and I went through the door. I saw other people who were tattooed. Though I don't know why I wasn't tattooed. And only in the last two years that I found out-- as a matter of fact, in the Holocaust Museum, that the excess of the people coming in was so high that they didn't have the time to tattoo everybody. It tooks-- it takes time to tattoo that number. So they let people go through with the Hungarian Jews. So I am one of those Hungarian Jews not tattooed. Not that I would have removed that, but I haven't got a tattoo.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to back up for one second. You talked about, when you arrived, the process that you went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that they took out the gold teeth.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that done?

speaker: interviewee
text: With a pair of pliers. Pulled out with a pair of pliers. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: No--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Nobody cared if there is a pain or there is no pain or there is blood or no blood or whatever. Somebody-- I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: And the process of delousing and--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was no delousing. The only thing, what they did, is that-- at that time, we had no-- thank goodness we had no lice. But the only thing what they did, they took off the hair. And from there onto the next room. And they gave us the one shirt and the uniform. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody ask what size are you are. Nobody ask if you are tall or short. Nobody ask if you are needing a size 10 or a size 12 or a size 8 shoes. They give you a shoe. And hopefully, if it didn't fit you, somewhere with somebody, you had to make an exchange. Or you had to wear it. I mean, that was it. And then we marched towards the camp, which wasn't further than maybe two miles from the railroad tracks. And that's Birkenau. And the reason why I mentioned the twin camp, the [GERMAN], was immediately adjacent to our camp. And we had a full view of what's happening there. Now, to describe the camp, I don't think so that you want I should describe it to you, how the campus basically was built as a-- Birkenau, where we went, that camp was originally-- all the barracks were prefabricated horse barns. And I can tell you only that I was in Barrack 21, in Camp B. And immediately adjacent to us was-- was the [GERMAN], the twin lager. And it was 20 if memory was-- I think so it's 22-- 23 barracks. One was the kitchen. So 22 barracks were living, people, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: 23 Individual--

speaker: interviewee
text: Barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: --barracks.

speaker: interviewee
text: 22 individual--

speaker: interviewer
text: House-- housing units.

speaker: interviewee
text: Housing unit, or this-- this-- these horse barns.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many in a housing unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I would like to say that our-- well, we were 922 in ours, 21.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry? Say that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: 921, in Barack 21. When we were ushered in, it was already sundown. To try to describe the amount of room, what we had, we had to put the hand on the shoulder once again. Line of fives, each side of the barrack, line of fives, marching in. Then put the hand on the shoulder of the guy in front of you. And then elbow to elbow-- elbow was a lot to put, you know, on your hip. And elbow to elbow. Then both ends of the barracks, there was one bulb, one-- one light, one bulb. And the bulb went out. And somebody else, when we later realized he's our kapo, that he's the one who is in charge of the-- of that barrack, to go to sleep.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how do you go to sleep? Because first of all, there is no bunks or no floor. It was hard floor of hard dirt. It wasn't, you know, like very, very strong. So we lie down. And we-- everybody get into-- you know the curled up fetal position? Is that the proper word? The fetal position.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the first guy had, basically, the best-- one of the best position or the best position. And the last guy had the-- one of the best positions or the best position. Because the only way that you are able to accommodate so many people is the person who was behind you, put his head on your-- on your tie. You know, the way you are semi curled up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first person, nobody kicked him in the head. And the last person was able to kick the wall. So when you are young and you start to-- we used, always, the word in concentration camp, you never stole anything. You never did anything wrong. You organized. That was our magic word. You organized. So we organized, as kids, my brother and I. Well, we are the head of the line, the last of the line. We were faster than the rest of the people in that barrack. How long did it take you to figure that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: One night. The first night, we knew it. And don't forget that we had, already, a background once again. We had already the previous-- and camp backgrounds. We had already the previous organize yourself, get yourself into the best position. Like I will tell you after. So we slept there. And the next morning, way before the sun came up, same kapo put on the light and he started to holler, outside for appell. Appell is counting. We had no idea what does it mean. Line of fives, stand in front of the barrack and in between the two barracks. And we stood there and stood there and stood there. And finally, the SS guard came. And the magic number of 922 was counted. And only then we found out that that's not enough, that our barrack was 922. Every single barrack had a number, how many people in there. And as long that so many bodies was there, and the whole camp, that's when the whole 22 other barracks were body-wise OK, appell was over. The appell could last from half an hour to three hours, four hours. And you couldn't move. You had to stand. Not in attention. But you had to stand in that one position, line of fives, that the SS should be able to easily count.

speaker: interviewer
text: What if you couldn't?

speaker: interviewee
text: You got shot. There was no questions asked. Sometimes we had that-- by the way, in between barracks they had the barbed wire and the electrified wire. The electrified wire had a sign on it saying I can't remember, 7,000 of 12,000, well, I don't know, some kind of volt, whatever it was. It was-- we saw, sometimes, when people went berserk or weak, or whatever you want to call it, desperate, that they ran against the electrified wire, and which meant that 1/2 a minute to 3/4 of a minute they were ash. They were burned to death instantaneously.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see this? Did you see instances?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, plenty. Plenty. I mean, people just couldn't take it. I mean, younger, older. I mean, people went berserk.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then after the appell, we got-- we received our coffee. The coffee was ersatz. Ersatz means replacements. Europe does not have any coffee. So I don't have to tell you that they wouldn't waste even replacement coffee, that they made something from carrots, beets, etc, different kind of vegetation. We had the black stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a cup of coffee. And the same cup was used at lunch time. Lunch time we had a cup of soup. Now, once again, our own experience, my brother and I, once-- from the past, we learned one thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't rush to go for to get the soup. Because the further you wait, the further it is the bottom. On the bottom there is some kind of-- some-- something is there, not only the liquid. At night, appell again. Once again, the counting. After appell we received one slice of black bread, like pumpernickel bread. But till today, I'm convinced that they had sawdust in there. Because that slice was at least a 1/4 of a pound. The heavy, heavy stuff. Which was good, because it filled your stomach. But basically, that was our food-- food, day in, day out, seven days a week, work or no work, that was concentration camp food. Ersatz coffee in the morning. Ersatz soup at lunch, a slice of bread at night. That was the diet.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop for one moment.
